skill.name: python-sdk
skill.description: Guides developers using the inference.sh Python SDK to run AI apps, build agents, stream results, upload files, and define tools.
skill.summary: Build Python AI apps and agents with the inference.sh SDK.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported Markdown examples as shell execution and weak cryptography, and I found no prompt injection evidence. The audit confirms high risk because the skill grants broad Python Bash access and includes eval-based tool handling, plus network, secret, and file-transfer examples that can expose data if copied without safeguards.

high_findings.title: Unsafe eval in Tool Handler Examples
high_findings.description: The tool builder guide evaluates a tool argument directly in calculator handlers. Tool arguments can be influenced by model output or user prompts, so this pattern can execute arbitrary Python if copied into an application.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The eval calls are direct and consume call arguments without validation. The surrounding context is an agent tool handler, which creates a realistic user-controlled execution path.

high_findings.title: Broad Python Bash Permission
high_findings.description: The skill metadata allows Bash execution for Python commands. This is useful for SDK workflows, but it expands the skill capability to arbitrary local Python execution when the assistant uses the skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicit in the skill header. It is not malicious by itself, but it is a broad execution grant that materially increases impact.

medium_findings.title: Network Webhook and Secret Handling Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows webhook tools that call Slack and GitHub endpoints and attach stored secrets. These are legitimate SDK features, but copied examples can transmit sensitive content to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external endpoints and secret attachment are shown directly. The examples appear instructional, so the concern is misuse or unsafe copying rather than proven exfiltration intent.

medium_findings.title: File Upload and Download Patterns Can Expose Data
medium_findings.description: The skill documents automatic uploads from local paths, public file options, and result downloads. These SDK operations are expected, but applications need allowlists and user confirmation for sensitive files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-transfer behavior is explicit and central to the SDK. The risk depends on how callers select file paths and public options.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Tool Dispatch Examples
medium_findings.description: Several examples execute tool operations using model-supplied call names and arguments. The guides mention approval in some places, but do not consistently require allowlists or argument validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in multiple tool handlers. Some approval checks reduce risk, but examples still delegate execution to generic handlers.

medium_findings.title: Optional Internal Code Execution and Web Search
medium_findings.description: The skill demonstrates enabling internal code execution and web search for agents. These are legitimate advanced features, but they increase risk when combined with untrusted prompts or sensitive workspace data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code execution setting is explicitly enabled in examples. The documentation context is legitimate, so this is a capability risk rather than evidence of abuse.

low_findings.title: API Key Placeholders and Environment Access
low_findings.description: Examples show direct API key placeholders and recommended environment variable usage. This is normal SDK authentication guidance, but users should avoid hardcoding real keys.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholders and environment variables, which are expected for SDK docs. The residual risk is users copying direct-key patterns with real credentials.

low_findings.title: Static Shell and Weak Crypto Findings Mostly Dismissed
low_findings.description: Most external command and weak cryptography alerts are caused by Markdown fences, package installation examples, model names, URLs, hashes, or ordinary documentation text. I found no evidence of hidden shell payloads or cryptographic misuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation or placeholder SDK setup, not executable malware. This conclusion is limited to the reviewed files and does not remove the separate broad Bash permission risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Code Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: Calculator examples evaluate a string expression from tool call arguments. Replace this with a safe expression parser or a small allowlisted operation set.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic evaluation is direct and appears in runnable examples. It is the strongest confirmed static finding in the audit.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Webhook Calls With Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Webhook examples send tool output to external endpoints and can attach stored secrets. Use explicit destination allowlists and redact sensitive message content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly combine network destinations with secret usage. The destination examples are common services, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Transfer
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples upload local paths and download remote results to local files. Require path allowlists, file size limits, and user confirmation for sensitive data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and documented as SDK features. Risk depends on caller-provided paths and deployment policy.

content.user_title: Build Python AI Apps with inference.sh
content.value_statement: Developers need clear patterns for using inference.sh from Python. This skill provides SDK workflows for apps, agents, streaming, files, and tools.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and initialize the inferencesh Python package.
Explains sync, async, streaming, wait, and session execution patterns.
Documents agent creation with template agents, ad-hoc agents, and reusable skills.
Covers tool builder types, app tools, agent tools, webhooks, and approvals.
Provides file upload, download, attachment, and multipart upload examples.
Includes error handling, retry, batching, and web framework integration patterns.
limitations: Requires an inference.sh account and API key for live use.
The skill is documentation and does not include the SDK package itself.
Some examples need security hardening before production use.
App and model availability depends on the inference.sh platform.

use_cases.title: Integrate AI Apps in Python
use_cases.description: Use client run patterns to call inference.sh apps, handle results, stream progress, and manage failures.

use_cases.title: Build Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Create agents with tools, reusable skills, human approval, streaming callbacks, and multi-turn conversations.

use_cases.title: Automate Media and Data Pipelines
use_cases.description: Upload files, process batches asynchronously, reuse sessions, and download generated outputs for downstream systems.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Python SDK Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to show the minimum steps to install inferencesh, configure authentication, and run one AI app.

prompt_templates.title: Add Streaming Progress
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to convert my basic app call into a streaming workflow with status handling and clear errors.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Agent With Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to design an inference.sh agent with two safe tools, approval checks, and validated tool arguments.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-sdk skill to review my planned inference.sh integration for async scaling, file safety, secrets, retries, and observability.

output_examples.input: Create a quick start plan for the inference.sh Python SDK.
output: Install the inferencesh package and confirm Python 3.8 or newer.
Load the API key from an environment variable.
Run one app call and print the completed output.
Add error handling before using it in a service.

output_examples.input: Plan a safe agent tool workflow.
output: Define each tool with a narrow description and typed parameters.
Require approval for external network, file, or destructive actions.
Validate tool names and arguments against an allowlist.
Return structured errors when a tool call is denied.

output_examples.input: Explain file handling options for an AI image pipeline.
output_examples.output: The skill would compare automatic path uploads, explicit uploads, file attachments, result downloads, temporary files, and multipart uploads.

best_practices: Load API keys from environment variables or a managed secret store.
Use approval checks and allowlists for tools that touch files, networks, or code execution.
Limit async concurrency and set timeouts to control cost and reliability.
anti_patterns: Do not evaluate model-provided tool arguments as Python code.
Do not make uploaded files public unless the user explicitly needs public access.
Do not send agent outputs to webhooks without destination and content review.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps build Python integrations for inference.sh apps, agents, tools, streaming workflows, and file processing.

faq.question: Does it install the Python SDK automatically?
faq.answer: It provides installation guidance. The user or assistant still runs the package installation command when needed.

faq.question: Can I use it for asynchronous workloads?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async clients, parallel requests, controlled concurrency, streaming, retries, and web framework examples.

faq.question: Does it support agent tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents client tools, app tools, agent tools, webhook tools, internal tools, and approval flows.

faq.question: What security precautions are important?
faq.answer: Avoid eval, validate tool calls, protect API keys, restrict file paths, and review webhook destinations.

faq.question: Is it ready for production code generation?
faq.answer: Use it as a reference, but harden examples for secrets, file access, network calls, code execution, and input validation.
